description.narrative: Percentage of Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability (PEFA) indicators ranked A or B • Number of government bodies using IFMS system • Number of Audit units or committees in 20 Provinces

comment.narrative: 34% of Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability (PEFA) indicators ranked A or B • 3 government bodies using IFMS system • No audit units or committees in 20 Provinces

Key Output: Number of state entities at national, provincial and district levels adopt and implement the integrated finance management systems to reduce corruption
Target: PCaB to be rolled out in 6 additional provinces

Provincial Capacity Buidling Programme (PCaB) continues to support the Department of Finance (DoF) and through the project PCaB successfully rolled out IFMS in 13 provinces and 8 Provincial Health Authorities (PHA). This is a critical requirement under the PNG Public Finance Managment Act. The 13 provinces are East New Britain (ENB), West New Britain (WNB), Central, Oro, East Sepik, Jiwaka, Simbu, Madang, NCD, Manus, Eastern Highlands, Western Highlands and New Ireland

Key Output: Number of state entities at national, provincial and district levels adopt and implement the integrated finance management systems to reduce corruption
Target: PCaB to be rolled out in 6 additional provinces

description.narrative: 1) Area of land and marine habitat recognised by CEPA 2) Improvement in the management effectiveness of protected areas score for sites receiving support from the Biodiversity Fund 3) Number of direct project beneficiaries, of which >65% are women and youth 4) Status of institutional mapping and mandates with respect to protected area management 5) Financial Scorecard 6) Capacity of organisations as measured using the Capacity and Competency Scorecard 7) Biodiversity Fund structure and governance in place. indicated by:-• Governing and operational structures – including the Steering Committee and Technical Secretariat – are in place 8) Biodiversity Fund operationalisation through a sinking fund. GoPNG,GEF grant funds and the fund raising/resource mobilisation strategy 9) Number of protected areas accessing funding from Biodiversity Fund 10) Protected area management effectiveness 11) The generation and use of revenue streams for protected area management in the three selected protected areas (Sepik Wetlands, Mt Wilhelm and Kimbe Bay) 12) Gender responsive linkages between sustainable financing of protected areas and environmental protection, and well-being of communities 13) Number of lessons (identified and disseminated by the project) used in development and implementation of other protected areas.

comment.narrative: The three key weaknesses in the system are: i) insufficient and unreliable revenue streams; ii) lack of capacity to secure and manage financial resources; and iii) a clear framework for investment in this sector.

Output 1.1 Institutional Analysis and mapping of CEPA, Wards and LLGs Targets: 1. Stakeholders mapping developed 2. Analysis of policy and regulatory framework for PA financing developed 3. Roadmap on PA Financing developed
Output 1.2 Development of PA Business Plan and initial implementation supported Targets: 1. National Level PA business plan developed
Output 2.1 Establishment of the Biodiversity Fund Governing and operational structures Targets: 1. Institutional design of Biodiversity Fund initiated 2. Review of best practice for establishment of Biodiversity Fund 3. Review existing policies and legislation in PNG to enable the establishment of a Fund 4. Legal and operational structure options for the Fund developed
Output 3.1 Active role of women, children and other marginalized groups Targets: 1. Environmental and Social Management Framework updated 2. Gender action plan and gender analysis for pilot sites updated
Output 3.2 Mechanisms to catalyze revenue streams to deliver to selected PA tested Targets: 1.Indigenous Peoples Plan developed for pilot sites 2. Grant Proposals drafted
Output 3.3 Documentation of various practices by different organizations and lessons learnt from them will be disseminated Target: 1. PNG METT revised through a peer review 2. Publication of PNG METT 3. Interactive online tool to communicate the results of the new METT created
Output 3.4 Lessons Learnt through M&E of the project Targets: 1. Project Monitoring and evaluation framework reviewed and updated 2. PA forum established with first forum convened.
Project Implementation Review 1. PIR process is conducted 2. Data collection process is coordinated by the PM and implemented by team members 3. Draft PIR is submitted for review

The Project is in the progress of implementation of its activities and the results will be achieved towards the end of 2021. Most of the 2020 key activities will be completed by mid 2021 including - Institutional and Regulatory Review report (Output 1.1) shall be completed in March 2021, National PA finance and investment plan (Output 1.2), establishment of the Biodiversity Fund (Output 2.1) - to be accomplished in June 2021, final draft of SES management framework and Gender Analysis and Action Plan updated (Output 3.1), Indigenous Peoples Plan completed (Output 3.2), and PNG METT approved and field tested (Output 3.3).

Output 1.1 Institutional Analysis and mapping of stakeholders 1. Identified stakeholders signed agreement to demonstrated roles and responsibilities 2. Stakeholders trained on results of institutional/regulatory review and PA finance and investment plan 3. Stakeholders trained on PA management and financing 4. PA establishment process developed and shared with stakeholders 5. Case studies on PA management documented
Output 1.2 Business Plan for PA system of PNG developed and initial implementation supported 1. National level PA finance and investment plan developed and approved 2. National PA finance and investment plan implementation published 3. National PA finance taskforce for the plan implementation established 4. At least 5 feasibility studies for viable finance options developed
Output 1.3 Capacity of relevant organizations an institutions development to deliver improved protected area management and governance, transparency and accountability and financial management across the country. 1. National PA training programme established 2. Provincial workshops for PA established and management conducted 3. Provincial training for business training and financial management of PAs conducted 4. Study tour to learn PA best practice undertaken
Output 2.1 Biodiversity Fund governing and operational structures are established 1. National biodiversity Fund regulation/legislation approved 2. National Biodiversity fund established and launched 3. Fund administrator and investment manager hired 4. Offices of fund secretariat established and technical secretariat hired
Output 2.2 Fund Strategic and operational frameworks are developed 1. Fund strategic plan approved 2. Fund operational manuals approved
Output 2.3 Fund -raising strategy/ resource mobilization strategy for the Biodiversity Fund is developed and implemented 1. Fund resource mobilization strategy approved.
Output 3.1 Mechanisms to deliver funds to protected areas and for participatory natural resource management tested 1. First round of call for proposals completed 2. First grant disbursements made to selected grantees.
Output 3.2 Mechanisms to catalyse (or consolidate) revenue streams to deliver funds to selected protected areas tested 1. Indigenous Peoples Plan developed for pilot sites 2. Grant proposals drafted 3. Grant agreements for selected implementing partners signed 4. Climate Change vulnerability assessments completed for each pilot site
Output 3.3 Lessons Learnt from implementing partner organizations documented and disseminated 1. Revised METT for PNG adopted 2. National and provincial training on the revised METT provided 3. Series of case studies on PA success stories on management, governance and financing developed and disseminated 4. A video showcasing PA management in PNG for national and international audiences developed
Output 3.4 Lessons Learnt from project through M&E are used for overall knowledge management, enhancing synergies, sharing experiences and learning 1. Baseline capacity scorecards are completed 2. PA forum established with first forum convened
Output 4 Project Management 1. Day-to-day project management support provided 2. Draft PIR submitted for review

The Project is in the progress of implementation of its activities and the results will be achieved towards the end of 2021. The project has strategized to complete most of the 2021 key activities by mid 2022 because of the COVID 19 pandemic and limited consultations with government counterparts and stakeholders. This will include - PA establishment process developed and shared with stakeholders, and case studies on PA management documented (Output 1.1); National PA finance taskforce for the plan implementation established, and 5 feasibility studies for viable finance options developed (Output 1.2); Study tour to learn PA best practice undertaken (Output 1.3) will be completed in Quarter 2; National Biodiversity Fund established and launched (Output 2.1); Fund administrator and investment manager hired, and offices of fund secretariat established and technical secretariat hired (Output 2.1) will be completed in June 2022; Fund strategic plan and operational manuals approved (Output 2.2); Fund resource mobilization strategy approved (Output 2.3); First round of call for proposals completed and first grant disbursements made to selected grantees (Output 3.1); Grant proposals drafted, Grant agreements for selected implementing partners signed, and Climate Change vulnerability assessments will be completed for each pilot site with support from the responsible party in 2022 (Output 3.2); and a video showcasing PA management in PNG for national and international audiences to be developed in 2022 (Output 3.3).

Output 1.1: Four regional knowledge sharing events to present the results and implications of the following outputs under component 1: (i) Institutional and regulatory review; (ii) PA finance and investment plan; (iii) Results of the first PA Forum; (iv) PA Bill, (v) PA establishment and management process, guides and templates 1. Institutional and regulatory review knowledge shared with stakeholders 2. PA finance and investment plan shared with stakeholders 3. Results of the first PA Forum shared with stakeholders 4. PA Bill enactment and information shared with stakeholders 5. PA establishment and management process, guides and templates shared with stakeholders
Output 1.2: Design and implementation of the finance solutions proposed in the PA Finance and Investment Plan (Budget committed from 2021 AWP) 1. National level consultation process on the design, implementation arrangements for national level PA finance solutions identified in the PA Finance and Investment Plan 2. Establishment of a protected area finance task force for the implementation of the plan 3. Development of NEC submissions and draft legislation for each finance solution.
Output 1.3: The capacity of relevant organizations and institutions development to deliver improved protected area management and governance, transparency and accountability, and financial management across the country. 1. Implementation of PA management modules (developed under GEF 5 project) for PA stakeholders during PA Forum. 2. Study tour for key PA finance stakeholders and Fund Secretariat to learn best practice for PA management, governance and financing (Asian Parks Congress identified)
Output 2.1: The Biodiversity Fund’s governing and operational structures are established. 1. MOU and TOR for Fund is signed between the UN RC and Department of National Planning and Monitoring (DNPM) 2. Fund Secretariat staff are appointed and trained - Executive Director (NPSA Senior Programme Officer), Programme Manager (NPSA Program Officer), Finance Manager (NPSA Finance Officer) 3. Fund is launched internationally (UN Biodiversity COP identified) 4. Domestic awareness raising of the Fund’s vision, strategy and operational rules conducted at a national and regional level 5. The Fund office is opened and furnished
Output 2.2: The Fund’s strategic and operational frameworks are developed 1. Finalisation of the Strategic Plan and operational manuals for the Fund are adopted by the Board (Budget committed from 2021 AWP)
Output 2.3: A fund-raising strategy/resource mobilisation strategy for the Biodiversity Fund is developed and implemented. 1. Development and implementation of the resource mobilisation plan (Budget committed from 2021 AWP) 2. Funding proposals developed identified under the resource mobilisation plan. (Budget committed from 2021 AWP)
Output 3.1: Mechanisms to deliver funds to protected areas and for participatory natural resource management tested 1. Fund issues first call for proposals for PAs 2. Grants are issued under LVG modalities to Protected Areas
Output 3.2: Mechanisms to catalyse (or consolidate) revenue streams to deliver funds to selected protected areas tested. 1. Grant agreement with Responsible Party is signed 2. Ecological and economic baseline assessment for project target sites conducted in Year 1 3. Climate change vulnerability assessment for project target sites undertaken in Year 1 4. Livelihood assessment for project target sites conducted in Year 1 5. Business plans for prioritised PA financing actions and livelihoods conducted in Year 1
Output 3.3: In a learning process, the diverse practices that are being implemented by various partner organisations across the country will be documented and the lessons learned from them will be disseminated 1. Develop a video showcasing protected area management in PNG for national and international audiences. 2. Mid-term review of the project conducted [Prepare ToR, advertisement, issue contract to international and national consultants)
Output 3.4: Lessons learned by the project through participatory M&E are used for overall knowledge management, enhancing synergies, sharing experiences and learning 1. Assess capacity scorecards for CEPA and each partner province 2. Organise national PA Forum (currently under development and funded through 2020 AWP)
Output 4.0: Project Management 1. Day-to-day project management and support to assurance activities

comment.narrative:
Baseline for Activity Result 1: 0 Climate change vulnerability assessments 0 Adaptation plans in place 0 Community investment plans.
Baseline for Activity Result 2: 0 sustainable fishery ecosystems in the targeted Islands and Atoll communities 0 Food Security Investments in the targeted Islands and Atoll Communities.

Targets for Activity Result 1: • 21 climate change vulnerability assessments (CCVA) • 21 climate change vulnerability assessment plans (CCVAPs) • 21 awareness raising campaigns/community consultations • 21 investment priority plans collected from communities • 21 gender-responsive disaster response strategies (developed in vulnerable island communities) • 21 disaster response committees established.
Targets for Activity Result 2: • 9 resource assessment reports on fisheries ecosystems • 9 community’s trainings on reef and coastal management • 9 LMMAs established, registered and operational with approved management plans being implemented • 9 mangrove forest rehabilitation demonstrated • 9 resource assessment reports on food security • 9 community’s trainings on food security initiatives • 3 watershed rehabilitation demonstrated in communities adjoining target vulnerable islands • 9 adaptation measures against climate change in home gardens demonstrated in nine target communities • 9 food processing and preservation initiatives piloted in nine island communities (50% women).

Activity Result 1 • 12 climate change vulnerability assessments (CCVA) completed • 12 awareness raising campaigns/community consultations completed • 12 investment priority plans collected from communities.

Targets for Activity Result 1: • 1 climate change vulnerability assessments (CCVA) for Ware Island • 1 awareness raising campaigns/community consultation for Ware Island • 1 investment priority plans collected from Ware Island community • 21 reports (vol.1 CCVAPs and vol.2 Priority Investment Plans) validated during provincial workshops • 21 gender-responsive disaster response strategies reviewed during provincial workshops • 21 disaster response committees established • 21 disaster response trainings completed.
Targets for Activity Result 2: • 9 resource assessment reports on fisheries ecosystems • 9 community’s trainings on reef and coastal management • 9 LMMAs established, registered and operational with approved management plans being implemented • 9 mangrove forest rehabilitation demonstrated • 9 resource assessment reports on food security • 9 community’s trainings on food security initiatives • 3 watershed rehabilitation demonstrated in communities adjoining target vulnerable islands • 9 adaptation measures against climate change in home gardens demonstrated in nine target communities • 9 food processing and preservation initiatives piloted in nine island communities (50% women).

Activity Result 1 • 20 climate change vulnerability assessments (CCVA) completed • 20 awareness raising campaigns/community consultations completed • 20 investment priority plans collected from communities completed. • 20 draft CCAPs (Vol.1) Investment Priority Plans (Vol.2) completed 30 June. • 20 draft Disaster Response Strategies completed.

Targets for Activity Result 1: • 30 SGF sub-projects implemented in 21 target island communities • 5 provincial training reports on SGF process and Investment Plans • 11 Disaster Response Committees established and trained at LLG level • 21 training reports on implementation of Gender Responsive Disaster Response Strategies • 5 provincial CCVAP mainstreaming workshops.
Targets for Activity Result 2: • 9 resource assessment reports on fisheries ecosystems • 9 community’s trainings on reef and coastal management • 9 LMMAs established, registered and operational with approved management plans being implemented • 9 mangrove forest rehabilitation demonstrated • 9 resource assessment reports on food security • 9 community’s trainings on food security initiatives • 3 watershed rehabilitation demonstrated in communities adjoining target vulnerable islands • 9 adaptation measures against climate change in home gardens demonstrated in nine target communities • 9 food processing and preservation initiatives piloted in nine island communities (50% women).

Activity Result 1: • 91 sub-project proposals completed. To be implemented in 21 target island communities in 2023. • Design completed for 5 provincial training on SGF process and CCVAPs and Investment Plans. • Design completed for 5 provincial CCVAP mainstreaming workshops.
Activity Result 2: • resource assessments underway for 3 sites in Milne Bay on fisheries ecosystems.

description.narrative: 1. National policy developed with associated regulations for renewable energy. 2. Policy notes developed for senior decision makers to help with facilitating the installation of renewable energy-based electricity generation systems. 3. Number of provincial and national energy working group meetings organized and reports shared. 4. Number (proportion) of MSMEs in the project area having access to project-supported renewable energy systems (except biomass). 5. Renewable energy generation facilities (except biomass) in operation in the project area established with project support. 6. Proportion of MSMEs in the project area having access to project-supported renewable energy systems (except biomass). 7. Annual amount of electricity (in KW) generated from renewable energy sources established with project support in the project area. 8. Database and website developed and in operation. 9. Number of awareness raising initiatives on renewable energy. 10. Report of gaps in current licensing arrangements identified for improvement. 11. Improved energy legislation and regulations submitted to government of PNG.

comment.narrative: 1. National Energy Policy 2017-2020 requires specific policies in relation to renewable energy. No sub-national policies. 2. No clear licensing requirement for renewable energy systems. 3. No accreditation standards for renewable energy systems in Papua New Guinea. 4. Most public facilities either have no electricity generation or some generation using fossil fuels. 5. No appropriate market assessment available. 6. None to limited capacity in generating renewable energy in the designated project area. 7. None to limited access to renewable energy and energy efficiency systems in designated project areas.

Sub-output 1: Support provided at policy and strategy levels to the Government of PNG on inclusion of food and agriculture in renewable energy development. Target: - 2 x Policy Notes Developed for Senior Decision Makers to help with facilitating the installation of renewable energy- based electricity generation systems.. - 2 x Provincial and National Energy Sector Working Group meetings organized and reports shared. - 1 x website and database developed and in use. - 1 x Awareness raising initiative developed and implemented. - 1 x Report of gaps in current licensing arrangements identified for improvement. - 1 x Improved energy legislation and regulations submitted to the GoPNG.
Sub-output 2: Feasibility of/investment options for renewable energy generation systems assessed. Targets: - 1 x Feasibility study report for solar power generation sites in East and West Sepik Provinces. - 1 x Market assessment report outlines potential suppliers of equipment both at the domestic and international stage. - 1 x Impact assessment (report) of potential pilot sites and public institutions. - 1 x Report on alternative funding sources and brokering support opportunities explored for investment.
Sub-output 3: Solar power based renewable energy generating systems established in public facilities in West and East Sepiks Target(s): - 1 x Feasibility study reports for energy efficiency audits of selected sites. - 1 x Environmental impact and social assessment reports of selected sites. - 3 x Solar-power generating electricity facilities installed at selected public facilities. - 1 x Report of assessment of suitable options, sites, technical specifications and hosts for the installation of solar power generating facilities. - 1 x Report of public consultations held includes requirements and end-users expectations for solar-based power sources. - 1 x Report identifying baselines against which to measure user, peak-periods and energy efficiency. - 1 x Data modelling report to inform policy and regulation discussions.

Sub-output 1: - No policy notes developed for senior decision makers. However, an inception report has been completed for Solar Energy and associated regulations. - No provincial and national energy working group meeting organized due to Covid-19 pandemic restrictions. - No website and database developed for implementation yet due to a lack of PMU support caused by difficulty with recruitment of suitable candidates to be based in Wewak, East Sepik province. - No awareness raising initiative developed for implementation due to a lack of PMU support caused by difficulty with recruitment of suitable candidates to be based in Wewak, East Sepik province. - Commenced gap analysis on current licensing arrangements, yet to complete a report. - Yet to submit to government an improved energy legislation and regulations. This is pending the gap analysis and further stakeholder consultations.
Sub-output 2: - Commenced work on conducting feasibility studies. An inception report was completed. - Commenced work on market assessment for potential suppliers of equipment both at the domestic and international stage. - An impact assessment of the same sites identified by the feasibility studies above is pending finalization of site selection. - Commenced work on identifying alternative funding sources to assist with installation of the systems identified by the feasibility studies.
Sub-output 3: - Feasibility studies have commenced. This is the same work in sub-output 2 above. - Environmental impact and social assessment work has started. This is the same work in sub-output 2 above. - No solar-power generation systems installed yet. This is pending the completion of feasibility and environmental and social impact studies. - Public consultations could not start in 2020 due to restrictions caused by the Covid-19 pandemic and a lack of PMU support. - Work on identifying the baselines for energy use is delayed to start in 2021 due to a lack of PMU support. - Work has commenced on data modelling to inform policy and regulation. See inception report referred to sub-output 1 above.

Sub-output 1: Support provided at policy and strategy levels to the Government of PNG on inclusion of food and agriculture in renewable energy development. Target: - 2 x Policy Notes Developed for Senior Decision Makers to help with facilitating the installation of renewable energy- based electricity generation systems.. - 4 x Provincial and National Energy Sector Working Group meetings organized and reports shared. - 1 x website and database developed and in use. - 2 x Awareness raising initiative developed and implemented. One in East Sepik and one in West Sepik. - 1 x Report of gaps in current licensing arrangements identified for improvement. - 1 x Improved energy legislation and regulations submitted to the GoPNG. - 1 x Model accreditation standard prepared and shared for use. - 1 x Accreditation and certification system advisory notes developed and shared with government of Papua New Guinea. - 2 x Accreditation and certification system capacity building workshops organized.
Sub-output 2: Feasibility of/investment options for renewable energy generation systems assessed. Targets: - 5 x Feasibility study reports for solar power generation sites in East and West Sepik Provinces. - 1 x Market assessment report outlines potential suppliers of equipment both at the domestic and international stage. - 5 x Impact assessment (report) of potential pilot sites and public institutions. - 3 x Report on alternative funding sources and brokering support opportunities explored for investment. - 3 x Advisory notes on investment options for project support and partnerships including EU-Blended financing Prepared and used for project support and partnership negotiations. - 1 x symposium/trade expo fares on renewable energy technology solutions convened. - 2 x Outcome report(s) on possible financing models approached to support renewable energy demonstrations.
Sub-output 3: Solar power based renewable energy generating systems established in public facilities in West and East Sepiks Target(s): - 5 x Feasibility study reports for energy efficiency audits of selected sites. - 5 x Environmental impact and social assessment reports of selected sites. - 6 x Solar-power generating electricity facilities installed at selected public facilities. - 5 x Report of assessment of suitable options, sites, technical specifications and hosts for the installation of solar power generating facilities. - 20 x Report of public consultations held includes requirements and end-users expectations for solar-based power sources. - 2 x Report identifying baselines against which to measure user, peak-periods and energy efficiency. - 1 x Data modelling report to inform policy and regulation discussions. - 3 x Commissioning reports for relevant installed equipment and O&M plans. - 10 x Beneficiaries trained on maintenance, operations ad budgeting renewable energy installations, disaggregated by sex and age.

Sub-output 1: - 1 X policy note developed for solar sub-policy. The policy was developed and is undergoing review by the National Energy Authority - No provincial and national energy working group meeting organized due to the Covid 19 Pandemic BCP. However, discussions with government partners to convene the meetings in 2022 were initiated. - No website and database was developed. This activity will be undertaken in 2022. - No awareness raising initiatives were developed and implemented due to Covid 19 Pandemic restrictions. However, one on one consultations were held with relevant stakeholder to promote RE in both East and West Sepik Provinces. - No advisory note being prepared and shared with government of PNG partners, however, discussions were initiated with the relevant government agency. - This activity was not undertaken as the capacity building workshop will be determine by the above activity on accreditation and certification of Solar Photovoltaic systems.
Sub - output 2; - On track. Feasibility studies was conducted for a total of 7 sites in both the East and West Sepik Province. - A market analysis was conducted and the report yet to be shared. - A total of 7 impact assessment reports were conducted for the selected pilot sites in both East and West Sepik Provinces. - An draft assessment report for alternate funding sources and brokering support opportunities for investment is being developed and will be shared for review. - This activity will built on from the assessment report above. No advisory note was developed. - No symposium / trade fairs held due to Covid 19 pandemic restrictions. Activity moved to 2022. - No outcome report produced.
Sub-output 3: - 7 x feasibility studies reports for energy efficiency reports done for selected sites. - No environmental impact and social assessment report was done for the selected sites due to Covid 19 Pandemic restrictions. - No solar power generation electricity facilities were installed at the selected sites. However, systems designs were done for 7 sites and the contract awarded to a contractor to install the Solar generating facilities. - 7 x assessment reports of suitable options, sites, technical specifications and hosts for the installation of power power generation facilities. - No public consultations were conducted. However, 16 one on one consultative meetings were held with relevant provincial government stakeholders and beneficiaries. - 7 reports identifying baseline of which to measure user peak periods and energy efficiency were developed. - No reports were produced for data modelling to inform policy and regulations. - No commissioning reports were done for relevant equipment and O&M plans. This activity will be conducted after the installations of the solar panels. - No beneficiaries were trained on maintenance, operation and budgeting renewable energy installations, However, 8 PNG Power technical officers were trained on solar PV rooftop installations.

Sub-output 1: Support provided at policy and strategy levels to the Government of PNG on inclusion of food and agriculture in renewable energy development. Target: - 1 x Policy Notes Developed for Senior Decision Makers to help with facilitating the installation of renewable energy- based electricity generation systems.. - 2 x Provincial and National Energy Sector Working Group meetings organized and meeting minutes shared. One for East Sepik and one for West Sepik Provinces. - 1 x website and database developed and in use. - 2 x Awareness raising initiative developed and implemented. One in East Sepik and one in West Sepik. - 1 x Report of gaps in current licensing arrangements identified for improvement. - 1 x Improved energy legislation and regulations submitted to the GoPNG. - 1 x Model accreditation standard prepared and shared for use. - 1 x Accreditation and certification system advisory notes developed and shared with government of Papua New Guinea. - 1 x Accreditation and certification system capacity building workshops organized.
Sub-output 2: Feasibility of/investment options for renewable energy generation systems assessed. Targets: - 6 x technical designs reports for solar power generation sites in East and West Sepik Provinces. - 1 x Market assessment report outlines potential suppliers of equipment both at the domestic and international stage. - 6 x Impact assessment (report) of potential pilot sites and public institutions. - 1 x Report on alternative funding sources and brokering support opportunities explored for investment. - 3 x Advisory notes on investment options for project support and partnerships including EU-Blended financing Prepared and used for project support and partnership negotiations. - 1 x symposium/trade expo fares on renewable energy technology solutions convened. - 1 x Outcome report(s) on possible financing models approached to support renewable energy demonstrations.
Sub-output 3: Solar power based renewable energy generating systems established in public facilities in West and East Sepiks Target(s): - 6 x technical design reports for energy efficiency audits of selected sites. - 6 x Environmental impact and social assessment reports of selected sites. - 6 x Solar-power generating electricity facilities installed at selected public facilities. - 6 x reports of suitable options, sites, capacity load assessment, technical specifications and hosts for the installation of solar power generating facilities. - 8 x Report of public consultations held includes requirements and end-users expectations for solar-based power sources. - 6 x Report identifying baselines against which to measure user, peak-periods and energy efficiency. - 1 x Data modelling report to inform policy and regulation discussions. - 6 x Commissioning reports for relevant installed equipment and O&M plans. - 10 x Beneficiaries trained on maintenance, operations ad budgeting renewable energy installations, disaggregated by sex and age.

Output 1.1: JSB meets regularly and its resolutions are implemented jointly by the two (2) Governments Target: At least 65%
Output 1.2: The two Parliaments make joint decisions on the BPA and referendum process, including on post referendum. Target: 1 public statement by the Speaker. Target: 3 (mitigating measures, consultation process and team members identified)
Output 2.1: Both Governments agree on joint messages on the BPA, including referendum, and facilitate their dissemination. Target: 1,000 copies of joint messages distributed throughout Bougainville.
Output 2.2: Innovative and community led dialogues about a peaceful future for Bougainville. Target: 5,000 people directly reached.
Output 3.1: In partnership with DPAI, implementation of the recommendations of the weapons disposal report by the UN, including support to the set up and operation of a Joint Secretariat; identification of remaining weapons and monitoring of collection. Target: 1 resolution
Output 3.2: Support to the factional unification in Bougainville, including bringing the remaining outliers on board with the BPA and helping to implement and monitor the MOUs between the factions and the ABG. Target: three factions recognize and work with ABG to actively participate in the implementation of the peace process.
Output 3.3: Targeted support to disenfranchised youth, which may include support with community conversations, referral to trauma services, community development for improved social cohesion, support to war wounded. Target: 3 completed Youth Resource Centres providing services identified by regional youth associations.

January to July 2020
Output 1.1: JSB meets regularly and its resolutions are implemented jointly by the two (2) Governments Result: 1 JSB held in March 2020 with 12 meeting resolutions.
Output 1.2: The two Parliaments make joint decisions on the BPA and referendum process, including on post referendum. Result: 0
Output 2.1: Both Governments agree on joint messages on the BPA, including referendum, and facilitate their dissemination. Result: This was not achieved due to the Bougainville Regional elections, COVID and other factors
Output 2.2: Innovative and community led dialogues about a peaceful future for Bougainville. Result: This was not achieved due to the Bougainville Regional elections, COVID and other factors.
Output 3.1: In partnership with DPAI, implementation of the recommendations of the weapons disposal report by the UN, including support to the set up and operation of a Joint Secretariat; identification of remaining weapons and monitoring of collection. Result: Resolution minuted in the March 2020 JSB meeting
Output 3.2: Support to the factional unification in Bougainville, including bringing the remaining outliers on board with the BPA and helping to implement and monitor the MOUs between the factions and the ABG. Result: 1 faction was recognized. This faction is the Panguna Mekamui. They joined the Joint Weapons Disposal Secretariat in 2019 to become part of the peace process.
Output 3.3: Targeted support to disenfranchised youth, which may include support with community conversations, referral to trauma services, community development for improved social cohesion, support to war wounded. Result: The 3 youth resource centres were completed and officially handed over to the Secretary for Community Development in January 2020.
August to December 2020
Output 1: Key institutional and operational capacities at national and sub-national level are strengthened to support a successful post referendum process Result: Two vehicles procured to support NCOBA to effectively perform its coordination roles
Output 2: Increased awareness on the post referendum process and progress ensuring that both the population in and outside of Bougainville is informed Result: No joint key messages or awareness materials were released by ABG.
Output 3: Increased opportunities for an inclusive peaceful process for all stakeholders to participate in the Post Referendum Process and contribute to a future Bougainville
Indicator: 3.1.1 Outlier groups engage in post referendum process (SPB) Result = 3 different groups were engaged in Buin. They are the Mekamui 1 lead by Damien Koike, Mekamui 2 (those that have broken away from Mekamui 1) and Freedom Fighters.
Indicator: 3.1.2 Economic and Investment Summit leads to additional investment commitments and economic opportunities (SPB) Result = 0
Indicator: 3.1.3 Human Rights Forum develops a Bougainville Human Rights Action Plan (SPB) Result = 0
Indicator: 3.1.4 Human Rights are monitored during the post referendum period by the Human Rights and Gender BHOR committee forming a yearly report (SPB) Result = 0

Output 1.1: JSB meets regularly and its resolutions are implemented jointly by the two (2) Governments Target: At least 65%
Output 1.2: Key government institutions with responsibilities for BPA implementation and coordination between the two governments are enabled to implement their functions effectively. Target: Parliamentary Partnership Agreement in place for cooperation and collaboration between the National Parliament and the BHOR
Output 2.1: Both Governments agree on joint messages on the BPA, including referendum, and facilitate their dissemination. Target: 1,000 copies of joint messages distributed throughout Bougainville.
Output 2.2: Innovative and community led dialogues about a peaceful future for Bougainville. Target: 5,000 people directly reached.
Output 3.1: In partnership with DPAI, implementation of the recommendations of the weapons disposal report by the UN, including support to the set up and operation of a Joint Secretariat; identification of remaining weapons and monitoring of collection. Target: 1 resolution
Output 3.2: Support to the factional unification in Bougainville, including bringing the remaining outliers on board with the BPA and helping to implement and monitor the MOUs between the factions and the ABG. Target: three factions recognize and work with ABG to actively participate in the implementation of the peace process.
Output 3.3: Targeted support to disenfranchised youth, which may include support with community conversations, referral to trauma services, community development for improved social cohesion, support to war wounded. Target: 3 completed Youth Resource Centres providing services identified by regional youth associations.

Output 1: Key institutional and operational capacities at national and sub-national level are strengthened to support a successful post referendum process Result: Two vehicles procured and handed over to NCOBA on April 2021, assisting to effectively perform NCOBAs coordination role
Output 1.1: JSB meets regularly and its resolutions are implemented jointly by the two (2) Governments Result: 3 JSB meetings held in 2021. One JSB meeting was held on 5 February 2021 in Arawa, Bougainville, another JSB meeting on 5 July 2021 in Wabag, Enga province and final one on 9 December 2021 in Port Moresby.
Output 1.2: Key government institutions with responsibilities for BPA implementation and coordination between the two governments are enabled to implement their functions effectively. Result: Logistical and operational support is provided to the two governments to proceed with political consultation dialogue. The ratifcation process is still being discussed through JSB, JTT and joint political consultation dialogue meetings.
Output 2: Increased awareness on the post referendum process and progress ensuring that both the population in and outside of Bougainville is informed Result: Joint Communication Working Group, comprised of ABG and NCOBA officers are continuing working on joint key messages, awareness raising and communication strategy.
Output 2.1: Both Governments agree on joint messages on the BPA, including referendum, and facilitate their dissemination. Result: Joint Communication Working Group, comprised of ABG and NCOBA officers are continuing working on joint key messages, awareness raising and communication strategy.
Output 2.2: Innovative and community led dialogues about a peaceful future for Bougainville. Result: Community led Transition Dialogues have reached a total of 6552 individuals, nearly 49% of them women and 35% of them are young people in 2021.
Output 3: Increased opportunities for an inclusive peaceful process for all stakeholders to participate in the Post Referendum Process and contribute to a future Bougainville
Indicator: 3.1.1 Outlier groups engage in post referendum process (SPB) Result = 3 different groups were engaged in Buin. They are the Mekamui 1 lead by Damien Koike, Mekamui 2 (those that have broken away from Mekamui 1) and Freedom Fighters.
Indicator: 3.1.2 Economic and Investment Summit leads to additional investment commitments and economic opportunities (SPB) Result = The Summit date is currently not confirmed and delayed to a later period in 2022. The Summit will provide a forum for key stakeholders to discuss the economic and investment activities and potential of the ARoB and chart a roadmap
Indicator: 3.1.3 Human Rights Forum develops a Bougainville Human Rights Action Plan (SPB) Result = 1 Human Right Forum is conducted in December 2021, with engagement of key CSOs, ABG officers
Indicator: 3.1.4 Human Rights are monitored during the post referendum period by the Human Rights and Gender BHOR committee forming a yearly report (SPB) Result = The key ABG officers (30 participants, 14 women) were sensitized on Human Rights Framework through a training, conducted on 5-6 August 2021 in Buka, AROB. Follow-up work is continiued to implement human rights framework, indicating key responsibilities of Government institution to protect and observe human rights in Bougainville.
Output 3.1: In partnership with DPAI, implementation of the recommendations of the weapons disposal report by the UN, including support to the set up and operation of a Joint Secretariat; identification of remaining weapons and monitoring of collection. Result: This output indicator is completed in previous phase of the Project.
Output 3.2: Support to the factional unification in Bougainville, including bringing the remaining outliers on board with the BPA and helping to implement and monitor the MOUs between the factions and the ABG.
Result: 1 faction was recognized. This faction is the Panguna Mekamui. They joined the Joint Weapons Disposal Secretariat in 2019 to become part of the peace process.
Output 3.3: Targeted support to disenfranchised youth, which may include support with community conversations, referral to trauma services, community development for improved social cohesion, support to war wounded.
Result: Up to 40 young men and women from Konnou Constiuency have received training on conflict prevention, peacebuilding, security awareness and financial literacy. Further follow-up work on implementing peacebuilding initiatives of youth from Konnou will be undertaken in 2022.

Target 2022 Output 1.1: JSB meets regularly and its resolutions are implemented jointly by the two (2) Governments Target: At least 65%
Output 1.2: Key government institutions with responsibilities for BPA implementation and coordination between the two governments are enabled to implement their functions effectively. Output Indicator 1.2.2: Number of MPs and ABG staff inducted on human rights, gender equality, inclusive peacebuilding Target: Up to 60 MPs and ABG staff trained on human rights, gender equality and inclusive peacebuilding
Target: 1,000 copies of joint messages distributed throughout Bougainville.
Output 2.2: Innovative and community led dialogues about a peaceful future for Bougainville. Target: 5,000 people directly reached.
Output 3.1: Advancing implementation of inclusive BPA and post-referendum process through engaging outlier groups with respect to gender and human rights Target: 15% increase in level of knowledge by Government officials on key pillar of the BPA, post referendum processes, gender and human rights centred peacebuilding Target: 10% increase in confidence level among women, youth and church members on post-referendum processes Output 3.2: Support two governments to host the Bougainville Economic Investment Summit considering needs of the outlier communities Target: Economic Investment is successfully conducted and Summit resolutions are endorsed by both Governments

description.narrative:
Indicator 1.1.1 Number of draft new and/or strengthened laws and/or policies on ending VAWG, including DV/IPV, and/or gender equality and non-discrimination developed that respond to the rights of women and girls, particularly those facing intersecting and multiple forms of discrimination, and are in line with international HR standards
Indicator 2.1.3 Number of strategies, plans and programmes of other relevant sectors that integrate efforts to end VAWG, including DV/IPV, developed in line with international HR standards
Indicator 2.2.1 Proportion of supported multi-stakeholder coordination mechanisms established at the highest level and/or strengthened composed of relevant stakeholders, with a clear mandate and governance structure and with annual work plans
Indicator 3.3.5 Number of key informal decision makers and decision makers in relevant non-state institutions with strengthened awareness of and capacities to advocate for implementation of legislation and policies on ending VAWG and for gender-equitable norms, attitudes and behaviors and women and girls’ rights.
Indicator 4.1.2 Number of women and girls with access to programmes developed to integrate VAWG, including DV/IPV, response into SRH, education and migration services
Indicator 4.2.2 Number of women and girls’ survivors and their families, including groups facing multiple and intersecting forms or discrimination, that have increased knowledge of and access to accompaniment/support initiatives, including longer-term recovery services.
Indicator 5.1.1 Number of National Statistical Offices that have developed/adapted and contextualized methods and standards at national level to produce prevalence and/or incidence data on VAWG, including DV/IPV
Indicator 5.2.1 Number of knowledge products developed and disseminated to the relevant stakeholders to inform evidence-based decision making.

comment.narrative: Output 1.1 - Baseline: Draft Operational Framework for the National GBV Secretariat developed in 2019 Output 2.1 - Baseline: Interim GBV Secretariat in place since 2019 Output 2.2 - Baseline: Draft organizational structure of the Nat. GBV Secretariat developed in 2019; zero GBV secretariat established in provinces. Output 3.3 - Baseline: 6 MPS between 2018 and 2019 engaged in discussions on advocacy on GBV prevention. Output 4.1 - Baseline: 0 Output 4.2 - Baseline: 0 Output 5.1 - Baseline: NCDC case management system/data operation but limited Output 5.2 - Baseline: 0

Output 1: Number of draft new and/or strengthened laws and/or policies on ending VAWG, including DV/IPV, and/or gender equality and non-discrimination developed that respond to the rights of women and girls, particularly those facing intersecting and multiple forms of discrimination, and are in line with international HR standards
Targets: Endorsement and operationalizing of framework for GBV secretariat
Output 2.1: Number of strategies, plans and programmes of other relevant sectors that integrate efforts to end VAWG, including DV/IPV, developed in line with international HR standards.
Target: Endorsement of full establishment of GBV Secretariat
Output 2.2: Proportion of supported multi-stakeholder coordination mechanisms established at the highest level and/or strengthened composed of relevant stakeholders, with a clear mandate and governance structure and with annual work plans
Target: Finalization the Operational Structure and commencement of recruitment and training of 6 officers from the Department of Community Development and Religion and 20 officers from Provincial Gender-Based Violence Secretariat & Provincial Family and Sexual Violence Action Committees).
Output 3.3: Number of key informal decision makers in relevant non-state institutions with strengthened awareness of and capacities to advocate for implementation of legislation and policies on ending VAWG and for gender-equitable norms, attitudes and behaviours and women and girls’ rights
Target: 30 Members of Parliament, 200 electoral support staff and parliamentary staff, 200 officers in departments where their heads of department or agency is involved, 30 business leaders and CEOs and 3,000 of their staff and workers. Media awareness through radio, TV and print media will be 5,500,000 based on coverage of National Broadcasting Company estimates.
Output 4.1: Number of women and girls with access to programmes developed to integrate VAWG, including DV/IPV, response into SRH, education and migration services.
Target: Establish an effective support system to track IPOs in NDC and 6 targeted provinces.
Output 4.2: Number of women and girls’ survivors and their families, including groups facing multiple and intersecting forms or discrimination, that have increased knowledge of and access to accompaniment/support initiatives, including longer-term recovery services.
Target: Train 30 Village court magistrates and 30 police officers and 40 other officials in 6 target provinces
Output 5.1: Number of National Statistical Offices that have developed/adapted and contextualized methods and standards at national level to produce prevalence and/or incidence data on VAWG, including DV/IPV
Target: Establishment and strengthening of existing case management system in Kokopo and Lae: total of 30 people to receive direct support.
Output 5.2: Number of knowledge products developed and disseminated to the relevant stakeholders to inform evidence-based decision making
Target: Development of M&E system for the National GBV Secretariat.

Results at Output 1.1 = Draft Operational Plan revised but not endorsed by DFCDR management; Work in progress, final results to be achieved in 2021 Results at Output 2.1 = National GBV Secretariat Office established within DFCDR Office; 12 staff positions allocated but not endorsed by Department of Personnel Management; Work in progress, final results to be achieved in 2021 Results at Output 3.3 = Coalition of Parliamentarians on Ending GBV formed in August, 2020 with 20 members of parliament; Special Parliamentary Committee on GBV endorsed by National Parliament in November 2020; Results at Output 4.1 = GBV Information Management System (GBV IMS) developed and officially launched for the Royal PNG Constabulary (RPNGC) Family Sexual Violence Unit (FSVU); GBV IMS to be rolled out to the Courts and Justice Systems; Work in progress, final results to be achieved in 2021 Results at Output 4.2 = Scoping assessments of needs conducted but trainings not rolled out due to COVID-19; Work in progress, results to be achieved in 2021 Results at Output 5.1 = GBV Information Management System developed for RPNGC FSVU yet to be rolled out into provinces; Work in progress, final results to be achieved in 2021 Results at Output 5.2 = M&E specialist conducted and working with National GBV Secretariat to develop a national M&E framework; Work in progress, final results to be achieved in 2021

description.narrative:
Project Outcome 1: Legal and policy frameworks and arrangements promote gender balance in political decision-making bodies Output 1.1 Technical assistance provided to Government of Papua New Guinea institutions as required to strengthen women's political leadership, especially at national level. Indicator: Types and number of activities delivered, including coordination activities (PW)
Project Outcome 2: A cadre of interested, diverse and capable women political leaders is formed at national and sub-national levels. Output 2.1 Action research undertaken to track progress for women over time in the target provinces. Indicator: 1. Types and number of activities delivered, including coordination activities (PW) 2. Number of women supported to take on leadership roles at the local, district, provincial and national level (PW; MTDP III 3.5.2)
Project Outcome 4: Women are promoted as leaders in gender sensitive political institutions. Output 4.1: Work with Political Parties (PPs) and the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Commission (IPPCC) to build commitment of PPs to gender equality and Women's Leadership.
Indicator: Types and number of activities delivered (PW)
Output 4.2 Work with National Parliament to promote Women in Leadership and gender equality. Indicator: 1. Types and number of activities delivered (PW) 2. Evidence of gender responsive laws and policies (PW; MTDP III KRA 3.5)
Output 4.3 Build capacity of Department for Community Development and Religion (DfCDR) to lead this agenda from the Government of Papua New Guinea's (GoPNGs) perspective. Indicator: 1. Types and number of activities delivered (PW) 2. Evidence of strategies to promote women's participation at all levels of government (MTDP III 3.5 Sector Strategy 5)

comment.narrative:
Project Outcome 1: Legal and policy frameworks and arrangements promote gender balance in political decision-making bodies
Output 1.1 Technical assistance provided to Government of Papua New Guinea institutions as required to strengthen women's political leadership, especially at national level.
Baseline:5 MPs attend Pacific Regional MPs’ workshop on gender equality and women’s empowerment, 2018 and 2019
Project Outcome 2: A cadre of interested, diverse and capable women political leaders is formed at national and sub-national levels.
Output 2.1 Action research undertaken to track progress for women over time in the target provinces.
Baseline: # of women in leadership positions in PacWIP website update 2012
Project Outcome 4: Women are promoted as leaders in gender sensitive political institutions. Output 4.1: Work with Political Parties (PPs) and the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Commission (IPPCC) to build commitment of PPs to gender equality and Women's Leadership.
Baseline: Gender mainstreaming workshop with MPs and political parties, 2018 and 2019
Output 4.2 Work with National Parliament to promote Women in Leadership and gender equality.
Baseline: 1. Gender awareness and sensitization meetings and conferences for MPs between 2012 to 2019. 2. 0
Output 4.3 Build capacity of Department for Community Development and Religion (DfCDR) to lead this agenda from the Government of Papua New Guinea's (GoPNGs) perspective.
Baseline: 1. Capacity Development assessment of DfCDR, ODW and NCW, 2010 2. Gender mainstreaming workshop with MPs and political parties and women leaders in 2018 and 2019

Project Outcome 1: Legal and policy frameworks and arrangements promote gender balance in political decision-making bodies Output 1.1 Technical assistance provided to Government of Papua New Guinea institutions as required to strengthen womens political leadership, especially at national level. Target: 1 national event per year to WIL and gender awareness for parties
Project Outcome 2: A cadre of interested, diverse and capable women political leaders is formed at national and sub-national levels. Output 2.1 Action research undertaken to track progress for women over time in the target provinces. Target: Website updated profiling women in leadership positions based on the electoral cycle for national and provincial elections including Bougainville and Motu Koita Assembly.
Project Outcome 4: Women are promoted as leaders in gender sensitive political institutions. Output 4.1: Work with Political Parties (PPs) and the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Commission (IPPCC) to build commitment of PPs to gender equality and Womens Leadership.
Target: Parties members with capacity to engage and advocate for 20% for women in the political parties and in the Organic Law on Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates
Output 4.2 Work with National Parliament to promote Women in Leadership and gender equality. Targets: 1. 1 National event per year to promote gender equality issues and dialogue 2. Set up of gender research unit or support services for MPs.
Output 4.3 Build capacity of Department for Community Development and Religion (DfCDR) to lead this agenda from the Government of Papua New Guineas (GoPNGs) perspective. Targets: 1. Updated assessment of ODW 2. Finalisation of the Organic Law on Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Commission with inclusion of 20% quotas for women

Outcome 1.1 Target: 1 national event per year to WIL and gender awareness for parties Results: None but 5 provincial workshops were conducted in Milne Bay. Jiwaka, East Sepik, Mt Hagen and West New Britain
Outcome 2.1 Target: Website updated profiling women in leadership positions based on the electoral cycle for national and provincial elections including Bougainville and Motu Koita Assembly. Results: An online Registration Form was developed for women to register their interest in contending 2022 general elections. IPCCC will then use the information derived from the registration form to use as the database for planning their future interventions. So far 77 women have been registered.
Outcome 4.1 Target: Parties members with capacity to engage and advocate for 20% for women in the political parties and in the Organic Law on Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Result: None
Outcome 4.2 Targets: 1. 1 National event per year to promote gender equality issues and dialogue Result: None 2. Set up of gender research unit or support services for MPs. Result: None
Outcome 4.3 Target: 1. Updated assessment of ODW Result: None 2. Finalisation of the Organic Law on Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Commission with inclusion of 20% quotas for women Result: None

description.narrative:
Outcome 1: Systems and Capacities are in place for Successful Post Referendum Process
Indicators:
1.1. Number of joint communiques on post referendum issued by the Joint Secretariat 1.2. Evidence of joint decisions by two Governments on post referendum consultations, by implementation of resolutions 1.3. Level of partnership between the national parliament and BHOR on the ratification process supported by joint statement 1.4. Increase in the proportion of population with a sufficient understanding of the post-referendum process 1.5. Improved understanding of the BPA and post referendum process by wider PNG population 1.6. Number of outlier factions participating under the peace architecture

comment.narrative:
Outcome 1: Systems and Capacities are in place for Successful Post Referendum Process
Baselines:
1. Number of joint communiques on post referendum = 0 (2020) 2- Referendum Results (2019) 3- Evidence of joint decisions by two Governments on post referendum = 0 4- Agreement on ratification process = 0 5- Sufficient understanding on the Post Referendum Process = TBD 6- Two outlier factions not participating under the peace architecture

Outcome 1: Systems and Capacities are in place for Successful Post Referendum Process
Targets:
1- 4 communiques over 2 years 2- Implementation of all necessary resolutions to progress post referendum consultations 3- Ratification process agreed 4- 50% of Bougainville Population understand the post referendum process 5- 10% of population understand the BPA and the Post Referendum Process 6- 2 Outliers and communities around the last factions become part of the post referendum process

Output 1: Key institutional and operational capacities at national and sub-national level are strengthened to support a successful post referendum process Indicator 1.1.1: Joint Secretariat fully operational and equipped to perform its functions Baseline: Jan 2020 = 0 Joint Secretariat Target: 1 Joint Secretariat established and operational Results: 1 Joint Secretariat established and operational
Indicator 1.1.2: Independent legal and constitutional opinions provided to aid consultations and resolutions Baseline: 1 independent legal opinion of the ratification process (2020) Target: 1 supported review of the constitutional requirements for two governments to consider Result: This is pending the decision of the two governments. No names have been suggested yet.
Indicator 1.1.3: External Moderator contracted and providing mediation support if and when required Baseline: 0 External mediator Target: 1 contracted for duration of consultations Result: This is pending the decision of the two governments. The former BRC Chairman, Bertie Ahern has been recommended as the moderator.
Indicator: 1.1.4: Frequency of Post Referendum Joint Ministerial Consultations Preparation Team meetings Baseline: Jan 2020 = 0 Target: 20 Result: 0
Indicator 1.1.6: National Parliament ratification process agreed and disseminated Baseline: 0 Ratification process Target: 1 Ratification process agreed by both governments Result: 0
Indicator 1.1.7: Awareness Sessions on ratification process conducted in the National Parliament Baseline: Jan 2020 = 0% Target: 50% per Year Result: 0
Output Indicator 1.1.8: Number of Awareness Sessions conducted by Parliamentary Committees in the National Parliament on ratification process Baseline: 0 Target: 2 Result: 0
Indicator 1.2.2: Awareness session conducted with new ABG officials following the 2020 ABG election on BPA and post referendum process Baseline: 2020 = 0 Target: 1 for new MPs Result: 0
Indicator: 1.2.4 Key groups: Women, Youth and Churches conduct dialogue forums in their localities on Post Referendum for bottom-up feedback in the Bougainville leader’s Consultative forum. Baseline: 2020 = 0 Target = 1 per group, per annum Result: 0
Output 2: Increased awareness on the post referendum process and progress ensuring that both the population in and outside of Bougainville is informed Indicator: 2.1.1 Joint messages agreed by the Co-Chairs of Post Referendum Joint Ministerial Consultations Preparation Team and distributed throughout Bougainville Baseline: 2020 = 2 Joint Messages agreed (for referendum) Target: 1,000 copies of joint messages printed and distributed in Bougainville Result: 0
Indicator: 2.1.4 Number of post referendum awareness sessions staged outside of Bougainville Baseline: 3 = 2018 Target: 6 (3 in 2021 and 3 in 2022) Result: 0
Indicator: 2.1.5 Number of Awareness Adverts aired across Papua New Guinea for Papua New Guinean’s to understand the post referendum process and progress addressing any questions and concerns Baseline: 1 = 2019 Target: 2 National Awareness Advert aired across Papua New Guinea via television, radio, and newspaper Result: 0
Output 3: Increased opportunities for an inclusive peaceful process for all stakeholders to participate in the Post Referendum Process and contribute to a future Bougainville Indicator: 3.1.2 Economic and Investment Summit leads to additional investment commitments and economic opportunities (SPB) Baseline: 2020 = 0 Target = 1 Result: 0

Outcome 1: Systems and Capacities are in place for Successful Post Referendum Process
Targets:
1- 4 communiques over 2 years 2- Implementation of all necessary resolutions to progress post referendum consultations 3- Ratification process agreed 4- 50% of Bougainville Population understand the post referendum process 5- 10% of population understand the BPA and the Post Referendum Process 6- 2 Outliers and communities around the last factions become part of the post referendum process

Result 2021 Output 1: Key institutional and operational capacities at national and sub-national level are strengthened to support a successful post referendum process Indicator 1.1.1: Joint Secretariat fully operational and equipped to perform its functions Baseline: Jan 2020 = 0 Joint Secretariat Target: 1 Joint Secretariat established and operational Results: National Coordination Office for Bougainville Affairs (NCOBA) and ABG Department of Idependence Mission Implementation (DoMI) are acting on behalf of the Joint Secretariat. Two vehicles procured and handed over to NCOBA on April 2021, assisting to effectively perform NCOBAs coordination role. IT and conference equipment in value of USD 40,000 were procured for ABG DoIMI.
Indicator 1.1.2: Independent legal and constitutional opinions provided to aid consultations and resolutions Baseline: 1 independent legal opinion of the ratification process (2020) Target: 1 supported review of the constitutional requirements for two governments to consider Result: This is pending the decision of the two governments. No names have been suggested yet.
Indicator 1.1.3: External Moderator contracted and providing mediation support if and when required Baseline: 0 External mediator Target: 1 contracted for duration of consultations Result: This is pending the decision of the two governments. The former BRC Chairman, Bertie Ahern has been recommended as the moderator.
Indicator: 1.1.4: Frequency of Post Referendum Joint Ministerial Consultations Preparation Team meetings Baseline: Jan 2020 = 0 Target: 20 Result: 3 JSB meetings held in 2021. One JSB meeting was held on 5 February 2021 in Arawa, Bougainville, another JSB meeting on 5 July 2021 in Wabag, Enga province and final one on 9 December 2021 in Port Moresby.
Indicator 1.1.6: National Parliament ratification process agreed and disseminated Baseline: 0 Ratification process Target: 1 Ratification process agreed by both governments Result: The ratifcation process is still being discussed through JSB, JTT and joint political consultation dialogue meetings.
Indicator 1.1.7: Awareness Sessions on ratification process conducted in the National Parliament Baseline: Jan 2020 = 0% Target: 50% per Year Result: 0
Output Indicator 1.1.8: Number of Awareness Sessions conducted by Parliamentary Committees in the National Parliament on ratification process Baseline: 0 Target: 2 Result: The ratifcation process is still being discussed through JSB, JTT and joint political consultation dialogue meetings.
Indicator 1.2.2: Awareness session conducted with new ABG officials following the 2020 ABG election on BPA and post referendum process Baseline: 2020 = 0 Target: 1 for new MPs Result: 0
Indicator: 1.2.4 Key groups: Women, Youth and Churches conduct dialogue forums in their localities on Post Referendum for bottom-up feedback in the Bougainville leader’s Consultative forum. Baseline: 2020 = 0 Target = 1 per group, per annum Result: 0
Output 2: Increased awareness on the post referendum process and progress ensuring that both the population in and outside of Bougainville is informed Indicator: 2.1.1 Joint messages agreed by the Co-Chairs of Post Referendum Joint Ministerial Consultations Preparation Team and distributed throughout Bougainville Baseline: 2020 = 2 Joint Messages agreed (for referendum) Target: 1,000 copies of joint messages printed and distributed in Bougainville Result: Joint Communication Working Group, comprised of ABG and NCOBA officers are continuing working on joint key messages, awareness raising and communication strategy. Once key messages confirmed, the national awareness advert will be aired.
Indicator: 2.1.4 Number of post referendum awareness sessions staged outside of Bougainville Baseline: 3 = 2018 Target: 6 (3 in 2021 and 3 in 2022) Result: Community led Transition Dialogues have reached a total of 6552 individuals, nearly 49% of them women and 35% of them are young people in 2021.
Indicator: 2.1.5 Number of Awareness Adverts aired across Papua New Guinea for Papua New Guinean’s to understand the post referendum process and progress addressing any questions and concerns Baseline: 1 = 2019 Target: 2 National Awareness Advert aired across Papua New Guinea via television, radio, and newspaper Result: Joint Communication Working Group, comprised of ABG and NCOBA officers are continuing working on joint key messages, awareness raising and communication strategy. Once key messages confirmed, the national awareness advert will be aired.
Output 3: Increased opportunities for an inclusive peaceful process for all stakeholders to participate in the Post Referendum Process and contribute to a future Bougainville Indicator: 3.1.2 Economic and Investment Summit leads to additional investment commitments and economic opportunities (SPB) Baseline: 2020 = 0 Target = 1 Result = The Summit date is currently not confirmed and delayed to a later period in 2022. The Summit will provide a forum for key stakeholders to discuss the economic and investment activities and potential of the ARoB and chart a roadmap

Outcome 1: Systems and Capacities are in place for Successful Post Referendum Process
Targets:
1- 4 communiques over 2 years 2- Implementation of all necessary resolutions to progress post referendum consultations 3- Ratification process agreed 4- 50% of Bougainville Population understand the post referendum process 5- 10% of population understand the BPA and the Post Referendum Process 6- 2 Outliers and communities around the last factions become part of the post referendum process

description.narrative:
Outcome 1: NACPA progressively implemented and monitored, including through law reforms Indicator - Internal public service anti-corruption processes working more effectively to prevent and expose corruption.
Outcome 4 - Network of community stakeholders in support of anti-corruption efforts mobilized. Indicator - More civic actors engaged in the fight against corruption

comment.narrative:
Outcome 1: NACPA progressively implemented and monitored, including through law reform. Baseline: World Bank corruption indicator = -0.91 Target: Improvement in World Bank corruption indicators
Outcome 4 - Network of community stakeholders in support of anti-corruption efforts mobilized. Baseline: TIPNG active but civil society scattered otherwise Target: : 5 civil society partners actively working on AC initiatives

Output 1.1: NACPA Secretariat supported to implement NACPA, including M&E and reporting requirements (NACPA POW#8) Target1: Complete NACPA framework under implementation Target2: PMNEC, TWG, sectoral groups meeting regularly to undertake effective oversight of NACPA implementation
Output 1.2: Key anti-corruption law reforms progressed (NACPA POW#1) Target1: FOI law passed Target2: PNG laws updated to implement UNCAC
Output 1.3: Guidelines and processes for new legal frameworks designed and implemented (NACPA POW#7) Target1: Supporting Regulations and guidelines produced Target2: Public servants and govt agencies complying with their duties under new regimes
Output 1.4: Corruption risk assessments completed and strategies designed in response (NACPA POW#3) Target: 10 agencies/units assessed
Output 1.5: Key anti-corruption institutions supported to implement their NACPA priorities in a coordinated and efficient manner (NACPA POW#2) Target: 5
Output 4.1: Non-state actors supported to undertake AC education, awareness and reporting Target1: 12 activities (approx. 4 per year) Target2: At least 10 major AC media stories/discussions related to Project support
Output 4.2: Catalytic small grant funding provided to support non-government and community anti-corruption initiatives Target: At least 10 grants

Output 1.1
PNG, as a State party to the UNCAC, is currently undergoing its second review cycle as part of the mechanism for the review of implementation of UNCAC (UNCAC Implementation Review Mechanism). This cycle focuses on UNCAC Chapters II (Preventive measures) and V (Asset recovery). UNODC is the UNCAC Secretariat and is supporting PNG’s review. The reviewers are from Burundi and Kyrgyzstan. Both the Government of PNG and the reviewers, with UNODC, have agreed on the country visit taking place during the week of 9 May 2022.
PNG, as one of 18 Pacific Islands Forum Leaders, further adopted the Teieniwa Vision (Pacific Unity Against Corruption) as the regional anti-corruption roadmap at the Leaders Forum in February 2021. The Teieniwa Vision, inter alia, includes development and review of the National Anti-Corruption Strategies and policies, and implementation arrangements, including within the national plans, through a participatory process which includes civil society, youth, private sector, Parliamentarians, media and other stakeholders.
The coordination meeting with the representatives of the key government partner organizations was held in November 2021, to discuss needs, priorities and activities for 2022.
Output 1.2 A joint UNODC-UNDP submission on PNG’s draft National Right to Information (RTI) Policy (2020-2030) was submitted on 28 October 2021. This submission responds to the consultation draft shared on the website of the Ministry for Information and Communication Technology and was at the request of the FOI Technical Working Group Chair, Mr. Flierl Shongol. This was on the back of a joint UNODC-UNDP webinar on right to information under the United Nations Pacific Regional Anti-Corruption (UN-PRAC) Project in 2020 that was held for members of the FOI Technical Working Group under the PNG Open Government Partnership (OPG) National Action Plan. It included FOI experts from the RTI Unit of Vanuatu, Office of the New Zealand Ombudsman and Office of the Australian Information Commissioner.
The RTI Policy will inform the process of drafting the FOI Bill which is among 10 key legal reforms identified and prioritized by the PNG Government and included in the NAPCA Programme of Work 1 - Legislative and Policy Framework. It is anticipated that the Project will focus on supporting finalization and implementation of the RTI Policy in late 2021 and early 2022, as the PNG national election in June 2022 make it unlikely that FOI legislation will be tabled and enacted until the new Parliament is elected and in place.
Output 1.3:
The implementation of the current Whistleblower Protection Act might be challenging due to the above-mentioned loopholes (see the details under the Output 1.2). That said, the Organic Law on the ICAC might provide more comprehensive protection of whistleblowers than the Whistleblower Protection Act. Read together, work can therefore still proceed to start progressing implementation of a whistleblower protection regime. In November 2021, the additional publication relevant to PNG was released by UN-PRAC, titled Whistle-blower Protections and Corruption Reporting in Pacific Island Countries. It includes a comprehensive review of the PNG Whistleblower Act 2020 that will inform further support to the implementation of the whistleblower protection legislation.
Output 1.4 The UNDP PNG Anti-Corruption Adviser conducted an initial discussion with UN-PRAC team to identify potential synergies amongst both projects, as UN-PRAC is also progressing work to support anti-corruption risk assessments and mitigation plans in Solomon Islands. This has, inter alia, included the knowledge product on corruption risks in public procurement. The Consultant working with Solomon Islands has been recruited by the UNDP PNG team to support this work.
Output 1.5 The establishment of the ICAC Appointment and Oversight Committees and the appointment process of the ICAC Commissioners was supported by the AC Project upon request of the DJAG and the ICAC Chairman. Fit and Proper Test had been developed for the appointment of the Commissioners and the Codes of Conduct for Appointment Committee and Oversight Committee was drafted upon request.
Output 4.1 AC team held two meetings with TI PNG as a key project non-governmental partner for public education and anti-corruption awareness activities. The discussions were focused mainly on the legal reform (challenges related to the ICAC legislation; developments related to the FOI Bill drafting and other FOI related support), and other key areas of the current TI PNG work (i.e. support to the Government, awareness activities, etc.). TI PNG has expressed their general interest to collaborate with UNDP in the project activities. UNDP has also started mapping potential NGO partners, including amongst youth groups, the private sector and the media.
To effectively engage civic sector actors not only from Port Moresby but also from the provinces, UNDP has started mapping anti-corruption actors across PNG during the reporting period. This will be an ongoing activity and UNDP will continue the mapping jointly with UNODC, based on the UN-PRAC project previous work on this area. The UNDP Anti-Corruption Adviser is seeking meetings with potential NGO partners, but has been impeded by the inability to travel in-country.
The International Anti-Corruption Day on 9 December was marked by the joint UNDP and UNODC visibility/awareness activity. Two anti-corruption posters with specific focus on PNG were developed by both teams and widely shared through the social media, and the public statement by UN Resident Coordinator a.i. was published.
Output 4.2 UNDP has started the recruitment process for the national staff who will support process of development of the procedures and implementation of these activities. UNDP initiated discussion with UN-PRAC project team to identify potential synergies amongst both projects, including in relation to the UN-PRAC team’s experience with accountable small grant funding to their own Pacific partners in order to ensure this Project can benefit from their lessons learned and good practice.

Output 1.1: NACPA Secretariat supported to implement NACPA, including M&E and reporting requirements (NACPA POW#8)

Q3:Target 1: FOI legislative instructions drafted Q4: Target 2: MPs consulted on the AC legislative reform
Output 1.3: Guidelines and processes for new legal frameworks designed and implemented (NACPA POW#7) Project Target 1: Supporting Regulations and guidelines produced Project Target 2: Public servants and govt agencies complying with their duties under new regimes
Q3: Target 1: Whistleblower Protection Regulations developed Q4::Target 2: Staff trained on whistleblower protection
