comment.narrative:
Output 1: Readiness of the Disaster Management Team members and the National Disaster Centre to prepare and respond to disasters enhanced 1). Disaster Management Team annual terms of reference, work plan and were renewed and accountability compact were adopted in Jan 2020; action items were monitored and resolved 2). 50 situation updates 3). 1 humanitarian response plan for COVID-19 4). 1 updated contingency plan for a La Niña event 5). 4 Disaster Management Team contingency plans (sudden onset, slow onset, civil unrest, refugee emergency) were created in 2019; sudden-onset and slow-onset were updated in 2020. 6). 5 Provincial Disaster Coordinators received needs assessment training in 2019, but no provincial trainings were carried out. 7). No formal engagement of civil-military coordination with the Disaster Management Team. 8). Disaster Management Team members adopted annual terms of reference and member compact that includes specific accountability to uphold the centrality of protection, address gender-based violence, and advocate for protection from sexual exploitation and abuse. 9). Protection Cluster strengthened by a dedicated cluster coordinator, regular meetings of cluster/sub-clusters, active participation in response and contingency planning. 10). Attention elevated on protection concerns under COVID-19 measures. 11), Protection was a focus of revised multi-sectoral response plan for COVID-19
Output 2: Updating the National Disaster Management 1, The Disaster Management Act has not been substantively updated since it was enacted in 1984.
Not Applicable
Not Applicable

Output 1: Readiness of the Disaster Management Team members and the National Disaster Centre to prepare and respond to disasters enhanced 1). Disaster Management Team annual terms of reference, work plan and compact on accountability are renewed and action are monitored 2). Monitor humanitarian situation and coordinate international humanitarian response, needs assessment, and humanitarian financing 3). Disaster Management Team contingency plans and 4W are current, reflecting preparedness to respond to emergencies 4). At least one PDC’s preparedness to respond to disasters and emergencies is improved 5). Papua New Guinea participates in Regional Consultative Group for Civil-Military Coordination 6). Gender mainstreaming is improved across project outputs
Output 2: Updating the National Disaster Management 1). Technical inputs to Disaster Management Act have been incorporated.

Output 1: Readiness of the Disaster Management Team members and the National Disaster Centre to prepare and respond to disasters enhanced
1. Disaster Management Team (DMT) • The DMT renewed its annual terms of reference and accountability compact, approved an annual work plan, and monitored and resolved 3 action items. • The DMT convened 10 regular meetings, 2 ad hoc meetings, and 1 donor meeting. The DMT Secretariat also met with NGO Development Council and regularly briefed United Nations Country Team and Security Management Team. • The project monitored and reported on 31 emergencies in addition to fortnightly updates on COVID19 and regular monitoring of African Swine Fever, Fall Army Worm, and movement of asylum seekers from West Papua.
2. Humanitarian monitoring, response coordination • The project tracked and regularly updated dashboard on international contributions to the COVID-19 response and vaccination rollout. • Coordinated needs assessments and humanitarian financing for king tide flooding across the country. • The project updated the COVID-19 4Ws (Who is doing What, Where, and for Whom) on a quarterly basis and facilitated the regular updating of the Pacific Logistic Cluster’s Pacific Logistics Mapping (PALM) platform.
3. DMT and clusters’ preparedness • As part of its preparedness work, the project led technical assistance on cluster management, the PALM platform, and the development of a Humanitarian Communications Plan for the UN Communications Group. • With Save the Children in PNG, the project initiated work on a national humanitarian cash and voucher assistance working group and with the CDAC Network on a national community engagement working group. These activities are also part of its activities to increase capacities for preparedness and response. • The project reviewed and updated DMT contingency plans for sudden- and slow-onset responses in preparedness for 2020-21 and 2021-22 La Niña events.
4. At least one PDC’s preparedness to respond to disasters and emergencies is improved • Activity postponed primarily due to COVID-19-related travel and gathering restrictions and secondarily due to staff incapacity
5. Civil-military coordination • Engagement was maintained but limited due to several factors, including COVID-19 related restrictions, personnel turnover at OCHA ROAP, and diminished participation by PNG NDC/RPNGC/PNGDF representatives
6. Mainstreaming gender • The project tracked participation in HAT project and DMT activities. • The project advocated for protection and inclusion in NCC response activities. • The project ensured protection and inclusion are objectives for preparedness planning.
Output 2: Updating the National Disaster Management
1). The project was able to accomplish one combined regional consultation for the MOMASE and Highlands regions. It was limited by COVID-19-related restrictions for travel and gatherings from conducting

Output 1: Readiness of the Disaster Management Team members and the National Disaster Centre to prepare and respond to disasters enhanced
1). Regular meetings of the Disaster Management Team • Disaster Management Team annual terms of reference, work plan and compact on accountability are renewed and action items are monitored.
2). Semi-annual joint meeting of the Disaster Management Team and National Disaster Committee • National partners’ preparedness to respond to disasters and emergencies is improved
3). Quarterly planning meetings of the Inter-Cluster Coordination Group • Disaster Management Team contingency plans and 4W are current, reflecting preparedness to respond to emergencies
4) Preparation for national community engagement working group • National partners’ preparedness to respond to disasters and emergencies is improved
5) Preparation for national cash working group • National partners’ preparedness to respond to disasters and emergencies is improved
6) Support monitoring of humanitarian situation and coordination of international humanitarian response in the country, including needs assessment and humanitarian financing • Disaster Management Team contingency plans and 4W are current, reflecting preparedness to respond to emergencies
7) Review and revision of the contingency plans • Disaster Management Team contingency plans and 4W are current, reflecting preparedness to respond to emergencies
8) Needs assessment training for national partners • National partners’ preparedness to respond to disasters and emergencies is improved
Output 2: Updating the National Disaster Management
1). Provide technical support and consultation to the National Disaster Centre for updating the National Disaster Management Act • Technical inputs to Disaster Management Act have been incorporated.

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

description.narrative:
Output Indicators:
Indicator 1: A number of best practices on inclusive and integrated landscape approach in Enga province of Papua New Guinea Indicator 2: Agricultural, forests and pastoral ecosystems where sustainable management practices have been introduced (ha) Indicator 3: Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions avoided (tonnes CO2eq) with EU support and Carbon sequestered (tonnes CO2) Indicator 4: A number of jurisdictions to integrate climate change adaptation / DRM and climate change mitigation into development planning, PIP, DSIP application process; Indicator 5: A number of capacity building trainings for provincial and district stakeholders on land use planning Indicator 6: A number of policies, regulations and plans on sustainable land use management at the national, provincial and district levels Indicator 7: A number of men and women are informed about climate change impacts and developed options for adaptation and mitigation Indicator 8: A number of High Conservation Value (HCV) and High Carbon Stocks (HCS) Risk Maps for Enga province Indicator 9: A number of provincial based climate change adaptation and mitigation initiatives supported Indicator 10: Number of smallholders reached with EU supported interventions aimed to increase their sustainable production, access to markets and/or security of land Indicator 11: Number of people in food insecurity and food crises reduced Indicator 12: A multi-stakeholder Coffee Platform in Enga Province to strengthen private sector partnerships Indicator 13: Number of small-scale farmers adopting sustainable and resilient agriculture practices accompanied by research entities Indicator 14: A number of beneficiaries (including women) increased their awareness on the use of sustainability standards for coffee production as well as capacity for traceability of coffee through supply chain Indicator 15: A number of policy and regulations to support Sustainable Coffee Production at the national and provincial levels

comment.narrative:
Output Indicators:
1. 0 2. 0 3. 0 4. 0 – in 2020 neither Enga Province nor any districts have climate change or DRR strategies in place 5. 0 6. 0 7. 0 8. 0 9. 0 10. 0 – smallholders have very limited access to extension services and support 11. Assessment to be undertaken at start of project 12. 0 13. 0 – there has been limited community outreach and advocacy in the province 14. 0 15. 0 16. 0 17. 0 18. 0 19. 0 – there has been limited action on land restoration 20. 0 21. 0
Resource Mobilization
Resources Mobilized. Contribution Agreement Signed with EU.

TARGETS FOR 2021: 1. A feasibility assessment of Enga landscape to determine baseline level on climate change, land use, agriculture as well as forestry carried out and recommendations considered for project implementation. 2. A Memorandum of Understanding to integrate climate change into provincial development planning framework signed and implemented 3. At least 50 representatives (30% female) of the provincial government benefited from the trainings on sustainable integrated landscape approach 4. A small grant disbursement facility established and at least three community-based projects on climate change supported 5. At least 4 case studies/success stories produced and widely disseminated 6. At least 2 community-based projects on climate change mitigation and adaption supported through the small grant facility
TARGETS FOR 2021: 7. A feasibility assessment of Enga landscape to determine baseline level on subsistence/family agriculture and recommendations considered for project implementation 8. At least 200 farmers and women groups benefited from capacity building trainings on climate resilient approaches and agricultural techniques 9. At least 3 best practices with small-scale family enterprises on the production of vegetable crops and access to finance
TARGETS FOR 2021: 10. A feasibility assessment of Enga landscape to determine baseline level on subsistence/family agriculture and recommendations considered for project implementation 11. At least 50 environmental officers strengthened their knowledge on conservation monitoring 12. At least 2 community-based projects on land/forest ecosystems conservation projects through small grants facility 13. At least 3 best practices with on community conservation initiatives and media products
TARGETS FOR 2021: 14. PEB meetings 15. Annual and quarterly work plans prepared on time 16. Level of fund disbursement (over 85%) 17. Timely submitted Annual programme and financial reports

Results for 2021: 1. A firm was contracted for conducting the feasibility assessment of Enga landscape to determine baseline level on climate change, land use, agriculture as well as forestry carried out and recommendations considered for project. The contract was delayed due to inability to find suitable candidates. The contract will be implemented in completed by Q2 2022. 2. A Memorandum of Understanding to integrate climate change into provincial development planning framework is drafted and is under review of the provincial government. 3. The capacity building consultants for all three components are hired who are developing the training material that will be delivered in 2022 to the target beneficiaries. 4. The small grants disbursement facility is established and had three rounds of announcement. There were two successful grantees in October round. There are other potential grantees in November and December rounds. 5. Two stories developed on the successful grantees. The project is also developing three animated videos covering project activities and for increased awareness raising about the project. 6. Two community-based projects on climate change mitigation and adaptation are supported through small grants facility.
Results for 2021: 7. A firm was contracted for conducting the feasibility assessment of Enga landscape to determine baseline level on climate change, land use, agriculture as well as forestry carried out and recommendations considered for project. The contract was delayed due to inability to find suitable candidates. The contract will be implemented in completed by Q2 2022. 8. The capacity building consultants for all three components are hired who are developing the training material that will be delivered in 2022 to the target beneficiaries. 9. The project is supporting agriculture development through small grants. The best practices will be developed next year.
Results for 2021: 10. A firm was contracted for conducting the feasibility assessment of Enga landscape to determine baseline level on climate change, land use, agriculture as well as forestry carried out and recommendations considered for project. The contract was delayed due to inability to find suitable candidates. The contract will be implemented in completed by Q2 2022. 11. The capacity building consultants for all three components are hired who are developing the training material that will be delivered in 2022 to the target beneficiaries. 12. Two community-based projects on climate change mitigation and adaptation are supported through small grants facility. 13. The project is supporting agriculture development through small grants. The best practices will be developed next year.
Results for 2021: 14. Two Project Steering Committee meetings were organized. 15. Annual and quarterly work plans were prepared on time. 16. Over 85% was committed and utilized in 2021. 17. All annual and quarterly reports are submitted on time.

description.narrative:
Outcome 1: By 2022,communities affected by conflict in the Highlands have increased capacity to promote and demand for peace and social cohesion. 1. Proportion of population who believe decision-making is inclusive and responsive, by sex and age 2. Conflict-related deaths per 100,000 population, by sex, age and cause 3. Number of new conflicts, annual
Outcome 2: By 2022,Highlands’ communities and households have improved resilience to manage risks and mitigate shocks from conflict and manmade/natural disasters 1.Change in coping strategies index (CSI/rCSI) 2.Proportion of informal employment in non-agriculture employment, by sex 3.Number of people participating in capacity development initiatives focusing on equitable and diversified economic growth
Outcome 3: By 2022, traditional/non-traditional leaders and service providers have enhanced knowledge and skills to integrate peacebuilding and human rights strategies into service delivery in conflict affected areas in the highlands 1. Proportion of victims of violence in the previous 12 months who reported their victimization to competent authorities or other officially recognized conflict resolution mechanisms 2. % increase in the proportion of female and youth representatives in local dialogue, conflict resolution and peacebuilding processes 3. Proportion of population satisfied with inclusive mediation processes
Outcome 4: By 2022, public institutions in the Highlands have people-centred, proactive and transparent/accountable systems to support effective leadership that promotes peace, security and human rights 1. Number of costed province/district development plans with MTDP III/SDG targets integrated into them 2. Proportion of population living in households with access to basic services 3. Percentage of population expressing satisfaction about quality of services provided by selected government and nongovernment institutions to combat corruption, prevent violence and provide access to justice

comment.narrative: 1. 0 capacity to demand for peace and social cohesion. 2. 0 level of capacity to improve resilience in managing risks and mitigate shocks from conflict and manmade/natural disasters 3. 0 knowledge and skills to integrate peacebuilding and human rights strategies into service delivery in conflict affected areas in the highlands 4. Public Institutions in the Highlands have lack of people - centered, proactive and transparent/accountable systems to support effective leadership that promotes peace, security and human rights.

Output 1: - 20 - 30 Inter-community small grants projects as confidence-building measure that reinforces inter-communal partnership and joint ownership - Training of 50 - 70 local traditional community/tribal leaders with leadership/conflict resolution skills - Conduct of facilitated mediation and follow-up community support (4 mediation teams)
Output 2: - Training of (50 - 70) provincial and district authorities with leadership/conflict management skills
Output 3: - Support post-conflict needs assessment to link at-peace communities with development assistance (as peace dividends) (Conflict needs assessment tool) Output 4: - Conduct regular tracking, monitoring & mapping of inter- and intra-communal conflicts to support early warning and triggering of early action intervention (4 quarterly and one annual summary reports)
Output 5: - Conduct of 8 inclusive District Peace Conferences to create platform for community-driven peace initiatives and generate momentum for peace - Inclusive and conflict sensitive sub-national integrated development planning (4 - 6 plans)
Output 6: - Reintegration of youth (incl. ex-fighters) by connecting to local labor opportunities
Output 7: - Field Coordination of Highlands Joint Programme (4 community engagement missions)

Output 1: - Support local faith-based conflict mediation mechanisms to address existing and new conflicts by establishing 4 mediation teams and ensuring they are fully functional - Training of 50 local traditional community/tribal leaders in conflict affected communities with leadership/conflict prevention and resolution skills - Reinforce internal organic community structures to support inclusive dialogue and decision making, conflict resolution in 4 communities
Output 2: - Establish 7 provincial peace and development dialogue networks for enhanced local governance, communication, information sharing and confidence-building - Organize 7 inclusive district peacebuilding forums for localized mutual learning - Coordinate post-conflict recovery support for 4 target communities based on differentiated assessed needs of affected community members - Highlands Joint Programme programme coordination support (4 community engagement missions)
Output 3: - Conduct detailed conflict analysis and listening exercise for Hela and Southern Highlands provinces (report) - Conduct conflict tracking and monitoring for early warning and early action (4 quarterly conflict tracking report & 1 annual summary report) - Conduct in-depth scoping exercise of existing formal law and justice institutions and interaction with traditional/informal justice mechanisms (1 Scoping report & 2 policy dialogue sessions)

description.narrative:
Outcome 1: Increased active participation of women in communities in community action to mitigate climate-induced conflict risks Outcome Indicator 1a: Proportion of women in target communities participating in/contributing to peacebuilding within communities by age. Output 1.1: Capacity of women community leadership particularly in climate-induced conflicts and in the development of climate adaptation measures is strengthened. Activity 1.1.1 Delivering training of women community leaders in awareness of climate-induced risks and local approaches/ to mitigate risks (UNDP) Output Indicator 1.1.1: Women’s self-rating of their understanding of climate-related security risks. Activity 1.1.2 Support community-led establishment of inclusive community civic spaces to facilitate collective decision-making (UNDP) Output Indicator 1.1.2: Women’s self-rating of their ability to contribute toward adaptation needs. Output Indicator 1.1.3: Proportion of community members in target communities who believe that community dialogue is inclusive for women participation and responsive to undertaken climate and conflict-sensitive actions, by sex and age.
Outcome 2: Communities and households have improved resilience to manage risks and mitigate shocks from conflict and human induced/natural disasters Outcome Indicator 2a: Assessed vulnerability of target communities. Output 2.2: Provincial policies and strategic frameworks are informed by climate-induced peace and security risks. Activity 2.2.1 Conduct of provincial and sub-provincial climate risk assessment in both Hela and Southern Highlands provinces (UNDP) Output Indicator 2.2.1: Number of communities targeted for support in development and implementation of community peace for development plans informed through provincial and sub-provincial climate risk assessments. Activity 2.2.2 Support provincial authorities in development of provincial climate and gender-sensitive peace and security strategies with costed provincial action

Baseline: 0 Target: 2

Project Document signed and funding secured
Funds for the project secured.

Output 1: - Deliver training of women community leaders in awareness of climate-induced risks and local approaches/ to mitigate risks - Conduct climate-gender-conflict baseline study - Support community-led establishment of inclusive community civic spaces to facilitate collective decision-making (10 - 20 grants)
Output 2: - Conduct provincial and sub-provincial climate risk assessment in both Hela and Southern Highlands provinces (2 assessment reports) - Support provincial authorities in development of provincial climate and gender-sensitive peace and security strategies with costed provincial action (2 strategies + facilitation of forums/events/workshops)

title.narrative: PNG-01002128 | PIMS-9906 Global Biodiversity Finance Programme- Papua New Guinea

description.narrative: The initiative “Global Programme on National Biodiversity Finance Plans” is an enabling activity that directly supports the implementation of the new biodiversity targets under the new Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), specifically it will focus on providing support to countries in developing: a) a national biodiversity finance policy and institutional review; b) an expenditure review to assesses spending related to biodiversity across all relevant sectors; c) an assessment of financial needs required to achieve the targets of the GBF; and, d) a National Biodiversity Finance Plan. This is underpinned by a transformational process to develop a shared vision and action agenda on financing nature in the country, closely involving the finance sector.

title.narrative: PNG-01002214 | PBF Empower Her-PNG Peace Initiative

description.narrative: The United Nations in PNG has been actively engaged in Women, Peace, and Security (WPS) and peacebuilding initiatives for more than a decade, with programming primarily centred on facilitating thesustained implementation of the Bougainville Peace Agreement in the Autonomous Region of Bougainville and promoting peacebuilding and conflict mitigation efforts in Hela and the Southern Highlands provinces,with a strong focus on women and youth empowerment.This project intends to empower women and girls and enhance the capacity of key actors to strengthen the Women, Peace, and Security agenda in PNG via targeted support to the policy frameworks and roll-out of impactful and measurable local initiatives in Hela and Southern Highlands provinces and in the Autonomous Region of Bougainville. The main aim of the project is to clearly develop a national WPS, implement specific outcomes in two pilot regions and importantly align these initiatives with on-going women, peace and development initiatives led by Government of PNG, UN agencies and other developmental partners.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

description.narrative: The Papua New Guinea (PNG) Parliament sits at the apex of national governance.In a Westminster parliamentary system like PNG’s, the executive branch sitswithin the legislative branch, making parliament one of the most importantsites of national discussion, debate, and action. The Bougainville House ofRepresentatives (BHoR) plays a similar role in the Autonomous Region ofBougainville (AROB) under their regional Constitution. Recognising the criticalimportance of both institutions to democratic governance in PNG, this Projectpromotes inclusive, accountable, transparent and equitable development throughMPs who are supported by Parliamentary Service staff by using the powers ofParliament.The project will also strengthen capacities of the staff of both parliamentsincluding the engagement of the public both in PNG and ARoB in theirunderstanding of the parliamentary processes.

title.narrative: Strengthening monitoring and financing capacity to accelerate the implementation of the SDGs and national priorities

description.narrative: Despite its abundant natural assets and biodiversity, Papua New Guinea faces significant development challenges as a poor extractive-based economy. These challenges include limitedprogress toward achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), inadequate financingfor the Medium-Term Development Plan IV (MTDP IV), and ineffective monitoring mechanismsfor tracking progress. The project aims to support the implementation of the MTDP IV with a keyfocus on the 10th Strategic Priority Area (SPA)-Climate Change and Natural EnvironmentProtection, establish a monitoring system for progress towards the goals and SDGs, identifyfinancing options using the Integrated National Financing Framework, and involve civil society, the private sector, and other partners in decision-making.

title.narrative: PNG-01003740 | GEF8 Accelerating PNG’s Sustainable Blue Economy through SG & IMA

description.narrative: Papua New Guinea (PNG) encompasses vast marine ecosystems with globally significant biodiversity. However, sustainable management of these ecosystems is constrained by population growth, coastal development, and climate change. This project will strengthen the protection and sustainable use of coastal and marine ecosystems through: 1) Implementing natural capital accounting, addressing cross-cutting upstream challenges through integrating the value of marine ecosystems into economic decision-making; 2) Establishing and improving Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) and adopting Marine Spatial Planning for improved policy coherence and better governance of coastal resources; 3) Addressing landscape level challenges in the tourism, food-fisheries, and urban development sectors through implementation of Nature-based Solutions and demonstrating public-private-community partnerships, with active engagement of local communities through Free, Prior, and Informed Consent (FPIC); and 4) Fostering knowledge sharing and learning by developing a community of practice and disseminating best practices both nationally and globally. The project will scale up successful pilots, leveraging past interventions to expand global environmental benefits. It will also coordinate capacity-building initiatives for key stakeholders, ensuring the long-term sustainability of conservation efforts. These efforts will be complemented by ongoing monitoring and evaluation to assess project impacts and support adaptive management. Through these activities, the project aims to place 3.9 million hectares of marine areas under improved management, restore 20,000 hectares of mangroves, move over-exploited fisheries to sustainable levels, and avoid 19 million tons of CO2e emissions. By building stakeholder capacity and benefiting 10,000 people (50% of whom are women). The proposed project aligns with the objectives of the Islands IP in terms of NCA, NbS, Integrated Planning and generation of financing options,

title.narrative: PNG-01004603 | KBF National Communications and Knowledge Management Strategy for NBSAP

description.narrative: Papua New Guinea, globally recognized for its biodiversity and endemism, has been engagedin a process of realigning its NBSAP Targets to the Global Biodiversity Framework. As part ofthis, representatives from several sectors are participating in a Technical Working Groupproviding their expertise. Although this work is proceeding as scheduled, there is a generalconcern amongst the TWG members: (i) limited knowledge and understanding of what theCBD is; (ii) why the need for NBSAP preparation; and (iii) what does the GBF aim to achieve?This is especially true with stakeholders from sectors outside of the more conventionalconservation partners. All of the TWG participants agree that in order for the next NBSAP to be successful, there is a need to improve the visibility of the NBSAP and elevate it importance amongst all concerned sectors at all levels in PNG. The proposed KBF Project aims to develop a s and Knowledge Management which will be implemented by the. This strategy willutilize digital technologies, improve dialogue amongst national decision makers and engagement at the subnational level with indigenous communities through practical consultations. Such a strategy will accelerate the implementation of the NBSAP and progresstowards the GBF Targets through an aligned whole government and a whole societyapproach.

title.narrative: PNG-01004812 | MPTF WMC II: Increased Voice for Women in Political Processes

description.narrative: The Women Make the Change II project (2025–2028) is the second phase of a joint initiative by UN Women and UNDP aimed at increasing women’s political representation and leadership in Papua New Guinea. The project seeks to ensure that by mid-2028, women are represented at the same or higher levels in political positions, legal and policy frameworks better enable women’s participation and women’s rights institutions and coalitions are strengthened to effectively represent women’s voices in decision-making.Over three years, the project focuses on four main areas:1. Building a cadre of diverse and capable women political leaders and coalitions.2. Reviewing and advocating for enabling legal and policy frameworks and ensuring their implementation.3. Enhancing the visibility and positive public perception of women leaders.4. Strengthening the capacities of women’s rights institutions, electoral bodies, and political institutions to promote gender-sensitive decision-making.The project is jointly implemented by UN Women and UNDP, with UN Women acting as the convening agency responsible for coordination, technical coherence and programmatic integration.

title.narrative: PNG-01004819 | MPTF Highlands Joint Programme Phase 2

description.narrative: The Highlands Joint Programme Phase 2 (HJP2) addresses the root causes of conflict, fragility, and underdevelopment in the Highlands region of Papua New Guinea (PNG), an area affected by inter-tribal violence, displacement, and socio-economic vulnerabilities. Building on lessons from Phase 1, HJP2 adopts an integrated, area-based approach to empower communities, strengthen governance, promote peace and social cohesion, and enhance resilience to climate risks. With a focus on the provinces of Hela, Southern Highlands, Enga, and Western Highlands, the programme aligns with PNG’s national development priorities and the United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Framework (UNSDCF), contributing primarily to Outcome 2 (Governance, Peace and Social Cohesion) and Outcome 4 (Sustainable Economic Transformation) while also contributing to Outcome 1 (Equality for Women and Girls); Outcome 3 (Inclusive Human Development); and Outcome 5 (Environment and Climate Change). HJP2 also supports the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), including SDG 5 (Gender Equality),SDG 13 (Climate Action), and SDG 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions).

title.narrative: PNG-01005532 | MPTF-UK Bougainville Facilitator Consultation Project

description.narrative: The Facilitator Consultation Support Project is designed to support renewed consultations between the Government of Papua New Guinea and the Autonomous Bougainville Government following the Melanesian Agreement. description.narrative: Papua New Guinea (PNG) stands at a critical juncture as intensifying multi-hazard and climate risks increasingly threaten national development gains, economic stability, and community wellbeing. Floods, landslides, droughts, cyclones, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and tsunamiscontinue to endanger lives and livelihoods—particularly for the nearly 80 percent of the populationdependent on climate-sensitive sectors such as agriculture and fisheries. These shocksdisproportionately affect women, youth, the elderly, and persons with disabilities, deepeninginequality and eroding social cohesion across vulnerable and remote communities.

description.narrative: * Cost definitions and classifications for programme and development effectiveness costs to be charged to the project are defined in the Executive Board decision DP/2010/32 **Changes to a budget affecting the scope (outputs), completion date, or total estimated costs require a formal budget revision that must be signed by the project board. In other cases, the UNDP programme manager alone may sign the revision provided the other signatories have no objection. This procedure may be applied for example when the purpose of the revision is only to re-phase activities among years.
